Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty unconventional. I'd block out chunks of time when I felt mentally ready rather than following a strict schedule. Being a visual learner, I'd create mind maps and diagrams from the course material, often spending hours on those even when I wasn't logging into the LMS. I probably studied around 24 hours a week, but a lot of that happened away from the computer with printed notes spread across my desk.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch the course materials because I was dealing with other stuff, then I'd have these intense catch-up sessions. I've always been someone who works in bursts rather than maintaining steady daily habits. Deadlines were usually what dictated when I'd really buckle down.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or if assignment details had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to download lecture slides or see if discussion posts had any useful insights from classmates. I wasn't someone who browsed the LMS casually—I went in with a specific purpose and then logged out pretty quickly.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became something I checked habitually like social media or email. It was more of a tool I used when necessary rather than a daily touchpoint. I'd remember to check it maybe a couple times a week, but it never felt integrated into my natural routine the way some students seem to have it.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the visual content at my own pace. I watched about an hour's worth on average, taking detailed notes and screenshots. The downloadable slides were also crucial because I could annotate them and turn them into my own study materials offline. Anything visual stuck with me better than walls of text.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums beyond making the minimum required posts. They felt like busywork to me, and I didn't find the conversations particularly enriching for my understanding. The reading lists I often skimmed or skipped entirely—I'd rather watch a video or find a YouTube explanation than read through dense academic texts. Time felt too precious to spend on things that didn't click with my learning style.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be assessed or if it genuinely helped me understand a difficult concept. I'd ask myself whether this would help me on the exam or the assignment. If something felt like it was just there to fill space or tick a box, I'd deprioritize it quickly. Practical application and visual clarity made things feel valuable.
Q8: Optional activities felt like nice-to-haves that I rarely got around to. Required ones had a weight to them—I knew I had to engage even if I wasn't in the mood. With optional stuff, I'd tell myself I'd come back to it later, but later never really came. There's only so much energy, and the required work always took priority.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download the slides and videos, then work through them on my tablet while sitting in a café or on my bed. I also used YouTube videos and external resources to supplement what the course offered. My physical notebooks are filled with diagrams and notes that no LMS could ever capture.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd be deep in revision using printed materials and handwritten notes, but my LMS activity would show nothing. I'd sometimes re-watch downloaded videos multiple times while making visual summaries. The system only saw me download something once, but I might have studied that same content for hours over several days.
Q11: All the time. I'd grab the lecture slides and videos in one session, then not log back in for a week while I worked through everything offline. It was more efficient for me to have everything saved locally rather than constantly going back online. My actual engagement with the material far exceeded what my login frequency would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely periods where I felt really sad and disconnected from everything, including the course. My attendance dropped because I just couldn't bring myself to show up some days. During those times, I'd still try to keep up with materials at home, but engaging felt like pushing through fog. The mindfulness exercises they offered actually helped me work through those patches.
Q13: Competing responsibilities hit me hard sometimes—work, family stuff, other courses all pulled at my attention. When I was fatigued, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those sections entirely for a while until I could find alternative explanations elsewhere. The stress was real but manageable.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have the LMS open while my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd scroll through materials without really processing them, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd watch a video but realize at the end that I'd zoned out for half of it. Those sessions felt unproductive even though they'd register as activity.
Q15: I definitely procrastinated on assignments even knowing they were important. Part of it was perfectionism—I wanted to do them well, so I'd wait until I felt ready. Other times I was just emotionally drained and couldn't face the work. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week. But I always pulled through eventually.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my activation switch. I'd have low activity for weeks, then suddenly become very focused as a deadline approached. My best work often came from those intense last-minute sessions where everything clicked into place. The LMS would show these spikes of activity right before due dates, which probably looked concerning but was just how I operated.
Q17: When I got good feedback on an assignment, it motivated me to engage more with similar content. My assignment scores were actually really strong, which gave me confidence that my approach was working despite the irregular attendance. Negative or critical feedback made me want to understand where I went wrong, so I'd revisit materials more carefully.
Q18: The course structure was fairly flexible, which suited me in some ways but also enabled my irregular habits. If there had been more frequent check-ins or smaller milestones, I might have engaged more consistently. The way it was set up allowed me to coast for periods and then catch up, which worked for my results but probably isn't ideal for building steady habits.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement much easier for me as a visual learner who prefers offline study. The video content was well-produced and genuinely helpful. What made it harder was when important information was buried in text-heavy sections or when the discussion requirements felt disconnected from the actual learning. A clearer visual roadmap of the course would have helped.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for something—maybe they're confused and clicking around trying to find answers, or they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. It could also be that they're engaging with the wrong things, spending time on peripheral content instead of what matters for assessments. Activity doesn't equal productive engagement.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. I had relatively low LMS activity but still earned a Merit because my actual learning happened elsewhere. I downloaded materials once, studied them extensively offline, and showed up prepared for exams. The system couldn't see my notebooks full of diagrams or the hours I spent with printed slides. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when students are active, what they click on, how long they spend on pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone is deeply focused or mindlessly scrolling. They definitely can't capture offline study, external resources, or the mental processing that happens away from the screen. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm genuinely curious about a concept and want to master it rather than just pass. For me, engagement looks like creating those visual summaries, making connections between ideas, and feeling satisfied when something clicks. It's internal and often invisible to tracking systems.
Q24: The LMS would never show my resilience—how I pushed through difficult emotional periods and still managed to perform well. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practices that helped me stay grounded or the genuine intellectual curiosity I brought to certain topics. My peer interactions were meaningful even if I only posted a few times online. The sadness I carried some weeks, and the strength it took to keep going anyway—none of that shows up in login data.
